content.decision_question: Should reform bundle multiple independent segregation-drivers (tracking age, admissions design, zoning/choice, legal exemptions) into a coordinated package to achieve comprehensive coverage, or should it isolate a single, more implementable lever even knowing that untouched channels will continue to produce stratification?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence on the relative magnitude of each segregation channel (tracking vs. geography vs. choice/exemptions) could help prioritize which lever yields the greatest marginal equity gain per unit of implementation effort, but it cannot resolve whether partial, channel-specific progress is an acceptable outcome versus a moral obligation to address all documented drivers simultaneously — that is a question about acceptable thresholds of remedial completeness.
content.tension: Evidence suggests Hungary's segregation is produced by multiple independent channels — early academic selection (T2/T6), admissions-mechanism bias (T3), and geographic/choice/legal exemption dynamics (T4) — such that any single-lever reform addresses only part of the problem. Bundling reforms (as T6 attempts by linking de-tracking to zoning controls) increases coverage but multiplies legal, administrative, and political complexity and risk of failure at multiple points simultaneously, whereas isolating a single lever is more tractable but leaves other stratification channels operating unchecked.
content.title: Comprehensive versus modular intervention scope
value_poles: administrative and political tractability of reform
